description: Oxytocin receptor gene variant associated with social behavior and empathy

GG.detail: Individuals with GG genotype tend to show higher levels of empathy, better social skills, and lower rates of social anxiety. Associated with higher oxytocin sensitivity.

AG.detail: Heterozygous carriers show intermediate levels of empathy and social behavior. Most common genotype in general population.

AA.summary: Lower empathy, higher stress response
AA.detail: AA genotype associated with reduced empathy, increased stress reactivity, and higher risk of autism spectrum traits. May benefit from social skills training.

description: APOE ε2 allele defining SNP, associated with reduced Alzheimer's risk

CC.summary: APOE ε2/ε2 - Strong protection against Alzheimer's
CC.detail: Homozygous APOE ε2 carriers have significantly reduced risk of Alzheimer's disease (up to 40% reduction). Associated with increased longevity and better lipid profiles.

CT.summary: APOE ε2 carrier - Reduced Alzheimer's risk
CT.detail: Carriers of one ε2 allele have moderately reduced risk of Alzheimer's disease. Better cardiovascular health compared to ε3 or ε4 carriers.

TT.summary: No ε2 allele present
TT.detail: Absence of protective ε2 allele. Risk depends on presence of ε4 allele at rs429358. Standard population risk for Alzheimer's.

description: APOE ε4 allele defining SNP, strongest genetic risk factor for late-onset Alzheimer's

TT.summary: APOE ε3/ε3 - Standard Alzheimer's risk
TT.detail: Most common genotype. Standard population risk for Alzheimer's disease. No increased or decreased risk from APOE gene.

CT.detail: One copy of ε4 allele increases Alzheimer's risk approximately 3-fold. Age of onset typically 5-10 years earlier. Consider preventive lifestyle interventions.

CC.summary: APOE ε4/ε4 - 8-12x increased Alzheimer's risk
CC.detail: Two copies of ε4 allele dramatically increase Alzheimer's risk (8-12 fold). Up to 50% lifetime risk. Earlier age of onset (60s vs 70s). Strong recommendation for cognitive health monitoring.

description: Alpha-actinin-3 deficiency, the 'sprint gene' affecting fast-twitch muscle fibers

CC.summary: Fast-twitch muscle advantage for sprinting
CC.detail: Functional ACTN3 protein in fast-twitch muscle fibers. Associated with elite sprint and power athletes. Better performance in short-duration, high-intensity activities.

CT.detail: Heterozygous carriers have intermediate muscle characteristics. Balanced between power and endurance capabilities. Most versatile athletic profile.

TT.summary: Alpha-actinin-3 deficiency, endurance advantage
TT.detail: Complete deficiency of ACTN3 in fast-twitch fibers. Associated with elite endurance athletes. Better performance in long-duration activities and improved muscle recovery from eccentric exercise.

description: Val158Met polymorphism affecting dopamine metabolism and cognitive function

GG.summary: Warrior gene - better stress performance, lower cognitive baseline
GG.detail: Fast COMT enzyme activity leads to rapid dopamine breakdown. Better performance under stress, lower anxiety, but slightly reduced working memory at baseline. Higher pain tolerance.

AG.detail: Intermediate COMT activity. Most common genotype with balanced cognitive and stress response characteristics. Moderate anxiety and pain sensitivity.

AA.summary: Worrier gene - superior cognitive function, stress sensitive
AA.detail: Slow COMT enzyme activity leads to prolonged dopamine availability. Enhanced memory and executive function, but higher anxiety and worse performance under stress. Lower pain tolerance.

description: MTHFR C677T variant affecting folate metabolism and homocysteine levels

GG.detail: Wild-type MTHFR with full enzymatic activity. Normal folate metabolism and homocysteine levels. Standard recommendations for folate intake.

AG.detail: Heterozygous carriers have moderately reduced enzyme activity. Slightly elevated homocysteine levels. May benefit from higher folate intake (especially during pregnancy).

AA.summary: Significantly reduced MTHFR activity (~30%)
AA.detail: Homozygous variant with substantially reduced enzyme function. Elevated homocysteine levels, increased cardiovascular risk. Requires higher folate supplementation (methylfolate preferred). Important for pregnancy planning.

description: Taq1A polymorphism affecting dopamine D2 receptor density and reward processing

GG.summary: Higher D2 receptor density, lower addiction risk
GG.detail: Normal dopamine receptor density. More efficient reward processing. Lower risk of substance abuse, obesity, and impulse control disorders.

AG.detail: Intermediate receptor levels. Slightly increased reward-seeking behavior. Moderate risk for addiction and impulse control issues.

AA.summary: 30-40% reduced D2 receptor density, high addiction risk
AA.detail: Significantly reduced dopamine D2 receptors. Blunted reward response requiring more stimulation. Higher risk of substance abuse, gambling, obesity, and ADHD. Benefits from structured reward systems and behavioral interventions.

description: IL-6 promoter polymorphism affecting inflammatory response

GG.summary: Higher IL-6 production, stronger immune response
GG.detail: Elevated baseline IL-6 expression. More robust inflammatory response to infection. Higher risk of chronic inflammation-related conditions (cardiovascular disease, autoimmune disorders). May benefit from anti-inflammatory diet.

CG.detail: Balanced inflammatory response. Most common genotype with standard immune function and moderate inflammation levels.

CC.summary: Lower IL-6 production
CC.detail: Reduced baseline IL-6 expression. Lower chronic inflammation but potentially weaker initial immune response. Reduced risk of inflammation-related diseases.

description: Brain-derived neurotrophic factor Val66Met polymorphism affecting memory and learning

CC.summary: Optimal BDNF function, enhanced memory
CC.detail: Val/Val genotype with normal BDNF secretion. Superior hippocampal function, better episodic memory, and enhanced response to cognitive training. Lower anxiety and depression risk.

CT.detail: Val/Met carriers have slightly impaired BDNF secretion. Mild reduction in memory performance and neuroplasticity. Intermediate anxiety and depression risk.

TT.summary: Reduced BDNF secretion, memory challenges
TT.detail: Met/Met genotype with 20-30% reduced activity-dependent BDNF secretion. Smaller hippocampal volume, reduced episodic memory, and impaired neuroplasticity. Higher risk of anxiety and depression. Benefits greatly from exercise and cognitive training.

description: APOA5 variant strongly associated with triglyceride levels

GG.detail: Wild-type APOA5 with optimal lipoprotein lipase activation. Standard triglyceride levels and cardiovascular risk. Responds well to dietary interventions.

AG.detail: Heterozygous carriers have 20-30% higher triglyceride levels. Increased cardiovascular risk. Should monitor lipid levels and consider low-fat diet.

AA.summary: Significantly elevated triglycerides
AA.detail: Homozygous risk variant with 50-70% higher triglyceride levels. Substantially increased cardiovascular disease risk. May develop metabolic syndrome. Requires aggressive dietary management (low-carb, omega-3s) and possibly medication.

description: Chromosome 9p21 variant, one of the strongest genetic risk factors for heart disease

GG.detail: Protective genotype with reduced risk of coronary artery disease and heart attack. Standard preventive care recommendations.

CG.detail: One copy of risk variant increases coronary artery disease risk by approximately 25%. Earlier age of onset for cardiovascular events. Recommend proactive screening and lifestyle modification.

CC.summary: Significantly increased CVD risk (~50-60%)
CC.detail: Homozygous risk variant with 50-60% increased risk of coronary artery disease. Much earlier age of onset (10-15 years younger). Strong recommendation for aggressive risk factor management (lipids, blood pressure, smoking cessation, exercise).

description: Nicotinic acetylcholine receptor variant affecting smoking addiction

GG.detail: Standard nicotinic receptor function. Lower risk of heavy smoking and nicotine dependence. Easier smoking cessation if started. Standard lung cancer risk for non-smokers.

AG.detail: One risk allele increases susceptibility to nicotine addiction. Higher likelihood of becoming a heavy smoker. Moderate difficulty with smoking cessation. Increased lung cancer risk if smoker.

AA.summary: High nicotine dependence, difficult cessation
AA.detail: Homozygous risk variant with altered nicotinic receptor function. Very high risk of heavy smoking and strong nicotine dependence. Significantly more difficult smoking cessation. Substantially increased lung cancer risk in smokers (~70-80% higher). May benefit from aggressive cessation support and pharmacotherapy.

description: FTO fat-mass and obesity-associated gene variant, the most replicated common obesity SNP

AA.summary: Highest obesity risk, ~1.6x increased type 2 diabetes risk
AA.detail: Homozygous risk variant associated with ~3 kg higher body weight and 1.6x greater risk of type 2 diabetes compared to TT. Reduced satiety signalling and altered energy homeostasis. Benefits from structured physical activity and caloric awareness.

AT.summary: Moderately increased obesity and T2D risk
AT.detail: Heterozygous carriers have intermediate increased risk (~1.3x T2D risk). One copy of the risk allele leads to modest increases in BMI. Lifestyle interventions are especially effective.

TT.summary: Lower obesity and type 2 diabetes risk
TT.detail: Wild-type genotype. Standard population risk for obesity and type 2 diabetes. Normal FTO-mediated energy regulation.

description: MTHFR A1298C variant (second major MTHFR polymorphism) affecting folate cycle and homocysteine

AA.summary: Normal MTHFR A1298C function
AA.detail: Wild-type allele. Normal MTHFR enzyme activity at the 1298 position. Standard folate metabolism and homocysteine levels. No increased risk from this variant alone.

AC.detail: Heterozygous carrier of the C variant. Mildly reduced MTHFR activity. Clinically significant primarily when combined with rs1801133 (C677T) variant (compound heterozygote). Standard folate intake recommended.

CC.summary: Elevated homocysteine, increased cardiovascular risk
CC.detail: Homozygous C variant. Reduced MTHFR enzymatic activity leading to elevated homocysteine levels. Increased cardiovascular and neural tube defect risk, especially in the context of low folate intake. Active folate (methylfolate) supplementation recommended.

description: DRD4 promoter variant affecting dopamine D4 receptor expression and novelty-seeking behaviour

CC.summary: Increased novelty seeking and reward-driven behaviour
CC.detail: Homozygous C variant in the DRD4 promoter. Associated with higher novelty seeking, risk-taking, and impulsivity. Stronger dopamine-driven reward responses. Higher susceptibility to thrill-seeking activities.

CT.detail: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate novelty-seeking scores. Slightly higher reward-seeking behaviour compared to TT. Most common genotype in many populations.

TT.summary: Lower novelty seeking, standard impulse control
TT.detail: Wild-type DRD4 promoter. Standard dopamine D4 receptor expression. Normal novelty-seeking behaviour and impulse control.

description: CYP1A2*1F variant determining caffeine metabolism speed (fast vs slow metaboliser)

AA.summary: Fast caffeine metaboliser (CYP1A2*1F/*1F)
AA.detail: Homozygous inducible variant. Higher CYP1A2 enzyme inducibility, especially in smokers and those with high coffee intake. Faster caffeine clearance. In heavy coffee consumers, associated with a 1.6x higher CYP1A2 activity. Lower caffeine side-effect risk.

AC.detail: Heterozygous carrier. One copy of the inducible allele. Intermediate caffeine metabolism speed. Standard caffeine sensitivity for most people.

CC.summary: Slow caffeine metaboliser
CC.detail: Wild-type CYP1A2. Slower caffeine clearance. Higher caffeine sensitivity — more likely to experience jitteriness, insomnia, or cardiovascular effects from coffee. May benefit from limiting intake to 1-2 cups/day.

description: Mu opioid receptor A118G variant (N40D) affecting opioid analgesia and addiction susceptibility

AA.summary: Standard opioid response and addiction risk
AA.detail: Wild-type mu opioid receptor. Normal opioid analgesic response and standard risk for alcohol/opioid dependence. Naltrexone treatment has standard efficacy.

AG.summary: Stronger alcohol cravings; enhanced naltrexone response
AG.detail: Heterozygous G carrier. Associated with stronger alcohol cravings and slightly higher risk of alcohol dependence. Naltrexone treatment is approximately 2x more successful in alcoholic individuals with this genotype.

GG.summary: Higher post-operative pain; requires more opioid analgesia
GG.detail: Homozygous G variant. Higher pain scores following surgery and greater opioid analgesic requirements. Naltrexone is highly effective for alcohol use disorder in this genotype. Significant pharmacogenomic implications for pain management.

description: TCF7L2 intronic variant — the strongest common GWAS signal for type 2 diabetes risk

CC.summary: Lower type 2 diabetes risk
CC.detail: Wild-type TCF7L2. Normal Wnt signalling and beta-cell function. Standard type 2 diabetes risk. No elevated concern from this locus.

CT.detail: Heterozygous T allele carrier. Moderately impaired beta-cell insulin secretion. ~40% increased risk of type 2 diabetes compared to CC. Lifestyle interventions (diet, exercise) are highly effective at risk reduction.

TT.summary: ~2x increased type 2 diabetes risk
TT.detail: Homozygous T variant. Most strongly associated with impaired insulin secretion and T2D risk — approximately doubled risk vs CC. One of the most replicated common genetic risk factors for T2D (GWAS p-value ~1×10⁻⁴⁹). Strong recommendation for regular HbA1c monitoring and preventive lifestyle changes.

description: TP53 Pro72Arg variant affecting apoptotic efficiency, longevity, and chemotherapy response

CC.summary: Pro/Pro — better chemotherapy response, slightly longer lifespan
CC.detail: Homozygous Pro72 (minus-strand C). More efficient apoptosis induction. Associated with ~3 years longer lifespan in Danish cohort studies. Chemotherapy tends to be more effective. Slightly higher risk of HPV-associated cancers.

CG.summary: Pro/Arg — intermediate lifespan and chemo response
CG.detail: Heterozygous. Intermediate apoptotic activity. Moderate chemotherapy efficacy and longevity effect. Most common genotype in many populations.

GG.summary: Arg/Arg — slightly shorter lifespan, more chemo-resistant
GG.detail: Homozygous Arg72 (minus-strand G). Slightly reduced apoptotic efficiency. Associated with marginally shorter lifespan. Tumours with this genotype can be more resistant to certain chemotherapies. Common in populations with African ancestry.

description: MC1R D294H variant strongly associated with red hair, fair skin, and elevated melanoma risk

CC.summary: Red hair likely; substantially higher melanoma risk
CC.detail: Homozygous risk variant at MC1R D294H. Strong association with red hair, freckles, very fair skin, and poor UV tanning response. Significantly elevated melanoma risk. Strict sun protection (SPF 50+, protective clothing, regular dermatological surveillance) strongly recommended.

CG.summary: MC1R D294H carrier; intermediate pigmentation risk
CG.detail: Heterozygous carrier. May have red hair or lighter pigmentation. Increased melanoma risk compared to GG, though lower than CC. Regular skin checks and sun protection advised.

GG.detail: Common non-risk genotype. Standard pigmentation and UV response. Standard population melanoma risk.

description: CYP2C9*2 variant (Arg144Cys) reducing warfarin and NSAID metabolism — the second-most common CYP2C9 loss-of-function allele

CC.summary: Normal CYP2C9 metabolism
CC.detail: Wild-type CYP2C9. Full enzymatic activity for warfarin and NSAIDs. Standard warfarin dosing applies. No increased risk of bleeding from this locus alone.

CT.detail: Heterozygous CYP2C9*2 carrier. Approximately 20% reduction in warfarin hydroxylation. Requires moderately lower warfarin doses. Also associated with slightly increased risk of GI bleeding with CYP2C9-substrate NSAIDs (ibuprofen, diclofenac, naproxen).

TT.summary: CYP2C9*2/*2 — ~40% reduced warfarin clearance, higher NSAID bleeding risk
TT.detail: Homozygous CYP2C9*2. Substantially reduced warfarin clearance (~40%). Requires significantly lower doses to avoid bleeding complications. Clinically significant risk of GI bleeding with many NSAID substrates. PharmGKB Level 1A pharmacogenomic evidence.

description: CYP2C9*3 variant (Ile359Leu) — the most severe common CYP2C9 loss-of-function allele, causing near-complete warfarin metabolism impairment

AA.summary: Normal CYP2C9 activity
AA.detail: Wild-type CYP2C9*1. Full enzyme activity. Standard warfarin metabolism and NSAID clearance.

AC.detail: Heterozygous CYP2C9*3 carrier. Approximately 40-50% reduction in S-warfarin clearance. Requires significantly lower warfarin doses. PharmGKB Level 1A evidence — CPIC recommends dose reduction to 25-30% of standard.

CC.summary: CYP2C9*3/*3 — near-complete loss of warfarin metabolism
CC.detail: Homozygous CYP2C9*3. Approximately 90% reduction in S-warfarin clearance. Extremely sensitive to warfarin — only 10-15% of normal dose required. Very high bleeding risk at standard doses. Rare but clinically critical — major dose reduction and close INR monitoring mandatory.

description: VKORC1 -1639G>A promoter variant — the strongest single genetic determinant of warfarin dose requirement

CC.summary: High VKORC1 expression — standard or higher warfarin dose needed
CC.detail: Wild-type (G/G on forward strand). Higher VKORC1 enzyme expression. Requires higher warfarin doses to achieve anticoagulation. Less sensitive to warfarin — may need doses of 5-7 mg/day or more.

CT.detail: Heterozygous A carrier (G/A on forward strand). Intermediate VKORC1 expression. Moderate warfarin sensitivity. Typical therapeutic dose around 3-5 mg/day. Most common genotype in mixed populations.

TT.summary: Low VKORC1 — very high warfarin sensitivity, low dose required
TT.detail: Homozygous A variant (A/A on forward strand). Markedly reduced VKORC1 expression. Highly sensitive to warfarin — typically requires only 1.5-3 mg/day. Major bleeding risk at standard doses. PharmGKB Level 1A. Common in East Asian populations (~90% A allele frequency).

description: CYP2C19*2 loss-of-function variant — defines poor metaboliser status for clopidogrel (antiplatelet), PPIs, and many antidepressants

GG.detail: Wild-type CYP2C19. Normal activation of clopidogrel to its active metabolite. Standard response to PPIs and CYP2C19-metabolised antidepressants (citalopram, sertraline, amitriptyline). Standard dosing applies.

AG.summary: CYP2C19 intermediate metaboliser — reduced clopidogrel activation
AG.detail: Heterozygous *2 carrier. Reduced CYP2C19 activity (~50%). Moderately impaired clopidogrel activation. Higher platelet reactivity and modestly increased risk of cardiovascular events in patients on clopidogrel. PPIs may be slightly more effective. CPIC recommends considering alternative antiplatelet therapy.

AA.summary: CYP2C19 poor metaboliser — clopidogrel largely ineffective
AA.detail: Homozygous *2. Near-complete loss of CYP2C19 activity. Clopidogrel cannot be activated — substantially higher risk of stent thrombosis and cardiovascular events. CPIC Level A: alternative therapy (prasugrel, ticagrelor) strongly recommended. PPIs are more effective at lower doses. Very common in East Asians (~15% *2/*2).

description: CYP2C19*17 gain-of-function variant — defines ultra-rapid metaboliser status, reducing PPI efficacy and increasing antiplatelet effect of clopidogrel

CC.summary: Normal CYP2C19 — standard drug metabolism
CC.detail: Wild-type at the *17 locus. Normal CYP2C19 expression. Standard response to clopidogrel and PPIs at standard doses.

CT.summary: CYP2C19 rapid metaboliser — increased drug clearance
CT.detail: Heterozygous *17 carrier. Increased CYP2C19 transcription. Faster metabolism of clopidogrel yields modestly higher active metabolite. PPIs are metabolised faster — may need higher doses for acid suppression. Intermediate between normal and ultra-rapid.

TT.summary: CYP2C19 ultra-rapid metaboliser — strongest effect
TT.detail: Homozygous *17. Up to 2-fold increase in CYP2C19 expression. Fastest clopidogrel activation — potentially higher bleeding risk with antiplatelet therapy. PPIs require higher doses for adequate acid control. Common in African populations (~8% *17/*17) and Swedes (~10%).

description: SLCO1B1*5 variant (Val174Ala) causing reduced hepatic statin uptake — strongest known genetic risk factor for statin-induced myopathy

TT.summary: Normal SLCO1B1 — standard statin tolerability
TT.detail: Wild-type SLCO1B1 transporter. Normal hepatic uptake of statins (especially simvastatin, atorvastatin, rosuvastatin). Standard risk of myopathy. No dose adjustment required based on this SNP alone.

CT.summary: Moderately reduced statin uptake — ~5x myopathy risk
CT.detail: Heterozygous *5 carrier. Reduced OATP1B1 transporter function. Higher plasma statin concentrations, approximately 5-fold increased risk of myopathy with high-dose simvastatin. CPIC recommends considering lower dose simvastatin or alternative statin.

CC.summary: Severely impaired statin transport — ~17x myopathy risk
CC.detail: Homozygous *5. Severely impaired OATP1B1 function. ~17-fold increased risk of myopathy with simvastatin 80mg. Major risk of rhabdomyolysis. CPIC Level A: avoid simvastatin 40-80mg; use alternative statins with less SLCO1B1 dependence (pravastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin at reduced dose).

description: MCM6 -13910C>T variant controlling LCT (lactase) gene expression — the primary European-ancestry lactase persistence SNP

AA.summary: Lactase persistent — can digest lactose throughout life
AA.detail: Homozygous T allele (reported as A on minus strand). Maintains lactase expression into adulthood. Can consume dairy products without GI symptoms. The dominant genotype in Northern European-ancestry populations.

AG.detail: Heterozygous carrier. One persistence allele is sufficient for continued lactase production (dominant trait). Most individuals with this genotype tolerate dairy well, though some may experience mild symptoms with very high lactose intake.

GG.summary: Lactase non-persistent — likely lactose intolerant after childhood
GG.detail: Homozygous ancestral allele (C/C on plus strand). Lactase expression typically declines after weaning, as in most mammals. Likely to develop lactose intolerance symptoms (bloating, diarrhoea) from dairy consumption. Most common globally (~65% of world population). Low-lactose dairy or lactase supplements recommended.

description: Vitamin D receptor BsmI variant (intron 8) — widely studied polymorphism influencing vitamin D signalling, bone density, and immune regulation

CC.summary: bb genotype — lower VDR activity, reduced bone density response
CC.detail: Homozygous C allele (bb genotype). Associated with lower bone mineral density in some populations, particularly with low vitamin D intake. Some studies show higher risk of osteoporosis and colorectal cancer. Standard vitamin D supplementation recommended.

CT.detail: Heterozygous. Intermediate VDR expression and function. Moderate bone density response to vitamin D. Most common genotype globally.

TT.summary: BB genotype — higher VDR activity
TT.detail: Homozygous T allele (BB genotype). Some studies associate this with better bone mineral density maintenance and more efficient vitamin D signalling. May have a modest protective effect against certain cancers. Adequate sun exposure and vitamin D intake still important.

description: HFE C282Y variant — the primary cause of hereditary hemochromatosis (HH), the most common autosomal recessive genetic disorder in Northern Europeans

GG.detail: No HFE C282Y mutation. Normal hepcidin-mediated iron absorption regulation. Standard population risk for iron overload.

AG.summary: HFE C282Y carrier — mildly elevated iron indices
AG.detail: Heterozygous C282Y carrier. Typically asymptomatic, but may have slightly elevated serum ferritin and transferrin saturation. Very low risk of clinical hemochromatosis unless combined with a second HFE variant (H63D). Regular blood donation may be beneficial.

AA.summary: HFE C282Y homozygous — high risk of hereditary hemochromatosis
AA.detail: Homozygous C282Y. Responsible for ~80-85% of clinical hereditary hemochromatosis cases in Northern European populations. Progressive iron accumulation leading to liver cirrhosis, diabetes, heart failure, and arthritis if untreated. Regular phlebotomy (blood removal) is highly effective treatment. Early diagnosis dramatically improves prognosis.

description: HFE H63D variant — the second major HFE polymorphism; mild iron loading, clinically significant primarily in compound heterozygotes with C282Y

CC.summary: Wild-type HFE H63D — normal iron metabolism
CC.detail: No H63D mutation. Standard iron absorption and storage. No increased hemochromatosis risk from this locus.

CG.summary: H63D carrier — slightly elevated iron, low clinical risk
CG.detail: Heterozygous H63D. Often associated with mildly elevated serum ferritin. Clinically significant hemochromatosis is rare unless also carrying C282Y (rs1800562). Considered a low-penetrance risk factor.

GG.summary: H63D homozygous — mildly increased iron loading
GG.detail: Homozygous H63D. Associated with mild-to-moderate iron loading in ~4% of cases. Rarely causes full hemochromatosis phenotype on its own. Risk substantially increased if also heterozygous or homozygous for C282Y. Iron indices should be monitored periodically.

description: Calcitonin receptor Pro463Leu variant associated with bone mineral density and osteoporosis risk

GG.detail: Wild-type calcitonin receptor. Normal osteoclast inhibition by calcitonin. Standard bone mineral density and osteoporosis risk.

AG.detail: Heterozygous Leu463 carrier. Some studies associate the Leu allele with modestly reduced bone mineral density and slightly increased osteoporosis risk, particularly in postmenopausal women.

AA.summary: Leu/Leu — potentially reduced BMD, higher osteoporosis risk
AA.detail: Homozygous Leu463. Associated with lower bone mineral density in multiple studies of postmenopausal women. Higher risk of osteoporotic fractures. Adequate calcium, vitamin D intake and weight-bearing exercise are important preventive measures.

description: Prothrombin G20210A variant — the second most common inherited thrombophilia; increases prothrombin levels and venous thromboembolism risk

GG.detail: Wild-type prothrombin gene. Normal plasma prothrombin (Factor II) levels. Standard population risk for venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE).

AG.detail: Heterozygous G20210A. Elevated plasma prothrombin levels (~30% above normal). 2-5x increased risk of venous thromboembolism (deep vein thrombosis, pulmonary embolism). Risk multiplied by other factors (oral contraceptives, immobility, surgery). Found in ~2-3% of Europeans.

AA.summary: Homozygous prothrombin mutation — high VTE risk
AA.detail: Homozygous G20210A. Very rare. Much higher plasma prothrombin levels. Very high risk of recurrent VTE and thrombotic complications. Long-term anticoagulation often necessary. Genetic counselling recommended.

description: Factor V Leiden (R506Q) — the most common inherited thrombophilia in Europeans, conferring resistance to activated protein C

GG.summary: Wild-type Factor V — normal clotting regulation
GG.detail: Wild-type Factor V. Normal cleavage by activated protein C (APC). Standard risk for venous thromboembolism (DVT/PE).

AG.summary: Factor V Leiden carrier — ~3-7x VTE risk
AG.detail: Heterozygous Factor V Leiden (FVL). Mutant Factor Va resists cleavage by activated protein C. 3-7 fold increased risk of DVT/PE lifetime. Risk further amplified by OCP use (35x), pregnancy (7x), immobility, and surgery. Present in ~5% of Northern Europeans. Most carriers never develop a clot.

AA.summary: Homozygous FVL — 25-50x elevated VTE risk
AA.detail: Homozygous Factor V Leiden. ~25-50 fold elevated risk of VTE. Very high risk of recurrent thrombosis. Long-term anticoagulation recommended after a first event. Genetic counselling and cascade family testing strongly advised.

description: LDLR variant associated with LDL receptor function and LDL cholesterol levels — a contributor to familial hypercholesterolemia spectrum

CC.summary: Normal LDLR — efficient LDL clearance
CC.detail: Wild-type LDLR at this locus. Standard LDL receptor activity. Normal LDL cholesterol clearance from plasma.

CT.detail: Heterozygous T allele. Associated with modestly elevated LDL cholesterol levels in some populations. Intermediate cardiovascular risk contribution.

TT.summary: Reduced LDLR activity — elevated LDL, higher CVD risk
TT.detail: Homozygous T allele. Associated with reduced LDL receptor function and higher LDL cholesterol concentrations. Increased cardiovascular disease risk, particularly when combined with dietary risk factors. Statin therapy may be more important for this genotype.

description: C-reactive protein gene variant associated with baseline CRP levels and cardiovascular inflammation risk

CC.summary: Lower baseline CRP — reduced chronic inflammation
CC.detail: Associated with lower baseline C-reactive protein levels. Lower chronic systemic inflammation. Reduced cardiovascular disease risk mediated via this inflammatory pathway.

CT.detail: Heterozygous. Intermediate CRP production. Moderate inflammatory state and cardiovascular risk from this locus.

TT.summary: Elevated baseline CRP — increased cardiovascular risk
TT.detail: Associated with higher baseline CRP levels. Elevated chronic low-grade inflammation. Higher cardiovascular disease risk, particularly coronary artery disease and stroke. Anti-inflammatory lifestyle measures (exercise, Mediterranean diet, no smoking) especially beneficial.

description: Melanocortin-4 receptor variant — one of the most replicated common genetic risk loci for obesity and increased BMI

TT.summary: Standard obesity risk via MC4R
TT.detail: Wild-type MC4R proximal region. Normal melanocortin-4 receptor-mediated appetite regulation. Standard population risk for obesity from this locus.

CT.detail: Heterozygous C allele. Modestly impaired satiety signalling via MC4R pathway. Associated with ~0.2-0.3 kg/m² higher BMI and ~30% increased obesity risk. Responds well to dietary and physical activity interventions.

CC.summary: ~1.6x increased obesity risk — impaired satiety
CC.detail: Homozygous C allele. Each copy of C associated with ~0.22 kg/m² higher BMI and ~1.6x elevated obesity risk. Reduced satiety signalling. Higher risk of food overconsumption and associated metabolic complications. One of the most consistently replicated obesity GWAS loci (p < 10⁻¹²).

description: PPARγ Pro12Ala variant — protective against type 2 diabetes through improved insulin sensitivity

GG.detail: Homozygous Pro12. Wild-type PPARγ2. Stronger transcriptional activation in adipocytes. Higher risk of type 2 diabetes compared to Ala carriers. Most common genotype globally (~75-80% in Europeans).

CG.detail: Heterozygous Ala12 carrier. Reduced PPARγ2 transcriptional activity leading to improved insulin sensitivity. Associated with ~25% lower type 2 diabetes risk compared to Pro/Pro. One of the most-replicated T2D protective variants.

CC.summary: Ala/Ala — greatest insulin sensitivity, lowest T2D risk
CC.detail: Homozygous Ala12. Lowest PPARγ2 activity — most insulin-sensitive PPARγ variant. Lowest type 2 diabetes risk from this locus. Rare (~2-3% in Europeans). Also associated with lower BMI in some cohorts. Meta-analysis of 30,000+ individuals confirms protective effect.

description: Serotonin receptor 2A (5-HT2A) promoter variant affecting receptor expression and antidepressant treatment response

AA.summary: Lower HTR2A expression — possible antidepressant advantage
AA.detail: Homozygous A allele. Associated with lower 5-HT2A receptor expression in some brain regions. Some studies suggest better response to SSRI antidepressants (particularly citalopram in the STAR*D trial). Lower risk of certain psychiatric disorders in some cohorts.

AG.detail: Heterozygous. Intermediate serotonin 2A receptor expression. Moderate psychiatric risk and intermediate antidepressant response profile.

GG.summary: Higher HTR2A expression — higher depression risk
GG.detail: Homozygous G allele (T/T on some strand conventions). Associated with higher 5-HT2A receptor density. Elevated risk of major depression, anxiety disorders, and suicidality in some studies. May respond less well to SSRIs. Clinically relevant for antidepressant selection.

description: Serotonin transporter linked polymorphic region (5-HTTLPR) rs25531 A>G variant modulating the functional effect of the classic SLC6A4 promoter VNTR

AA.summary: 5-HTTLPR long haplotype (LA) — higher serotonin transporter expression
AA.detail: The A allele in combination with the L (long) VNTR allele defines the LA haplotype — the highest-expressing form. Associated with greater serotonin reuptake capacity, resilience to stress-induced depression, and better SSRI response in multiple studies.

AG.detail: Heterozygous. The G allele converts L to LG, reducing expression almost to S-allele levels. Intermediate serotonin transporter levels. Moderate stress vulnerability and intermediate antidepressant response.

GG.summary: Functional S-allele equivalent — lower transporter expression
GG.detail: Homozygous G allele. Combined with the L VNTR this creates the LG haplotype, functionally equivalent to two S alleles. Lower serotonin transporter expression, higher amygdala reactivity, greater risk of depression and anxiety under stress, and reduced SSRI efficacy. Robust finding replicated in hundreds of studies.

description: CLOCK gene variant influencing circadian preference, sleep duration, and metabolic consequences of circadian disruption

CC.summary: Standard circadian regulation
CC.detail: Wild-type CLOCK. Normal circadian rhythm strength and sleep-wake cycle timing. Standard metabolic risk from this locus.

CT.detail: Heterozygous T carrier. Modestly altered CLOCK gene expression. Some studies associate with slightly shifted sleep timing and mildly elevated BMI in shift workers.

TT.summary: Disrupted circadian timing — elevated obesity risk
TT.detail: Homozygous T allele. Associated with evening chronotype, shorter sleep duration, and higher BMI. Disrupted CLOCK function can impair metabolic regulation, increase appetite especially in the evening, and elevate risk of metabolic syndrome and obesity. Good sleep hygiene especially important.

description: Estrogen receptor alpha (ESR1) PvuII variant — widely studied polymorphism influencing bone mineral density and cardiovascular disease risk in women

CC.summary: PP genotype — higher BMD, some cardiovascular effect
CC.detail: Homozygous PP (C/C) genotype. Associated with higher bone mineral density and better response to estrogen-based therapies in some studies. Standard cardiovascular risk from this locus.

CT.detail: Heterozygous Pp. Intermediate bone mineral density. Most common genotype. No strong directional risk from this locus alone.

TT.summary: pp genotype — lower BMD, higher osteoporosis risk
TT.detail: Homozygous pp (T/T) genotype. Associated with lower bone mineral density in multiple meta-analyses, particularly in post-menopausal women. Higher fracture risk. Some studies suggest increased cardiovascular risk in older women. Adequate calcium, vitamin D, and weight-bearing exercise recommended.

description: PCSK9 loss-of-function associated variant — reduced PCSK9 activity leads to increased LDL receptor recycling and lower LDL cholesterol

AA.summary: Standard PCSK9 activity — normal LDL levels
AA.detail: Wild-type PCSK9 reference allele. Normal PCSK9-mediated LDLR degradation. Standard LDL cholesterol levels and cardiovascular risk from this locus.

AC.summary: Intermediate PCSK9 activity — moderately lower LDL
AC.detail: Heterozygous C allele. Partial reduction in PCSK9 function. Modestly lower LDL cholesterol. Intermediate cardiovascular protection.

CC.summary: Reduced PCSK9 activity — lower LDL, protective CVD effect
CC.detail: Homozygous C allele. Reduced PCSK9 activity means fewer LDL receptors are degraded — more LDLRs remain on hepatocyte surfaces, clearing more LDL from circulation. Associated with 15-20% lower LDL cholesterol and substantial reduction in coronary heart disease risk. PCSK9 inhibitors (evolocumab, alirocumab) are designed to mimic this effect pharmacologically.

sources.name: PMID 16554528 — Cohen et al. PCSK9 LOF

description: TNF-α -308G>A promoter variant — the most studied TNF polymorphism, associated with inflammatory diseases and immune response strength

GG.detail: Wild-type TNF promoter. Normal TNF-α expression. Standard inflammatory response and immune regulation. No increased autoimmune or inflammatory disease risk from this variant.

AG.summary: Moderately elevated TNF-α — higher autoimmune risk
AG.detail: Heterozygous A carrier. Increased TNF-α promoter activity. Higher baseline and inducible TNF-α production. Associated with increased risk of rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, Crohn's disease, and sepsis severity.

AA.summary: Highest TNF-α production — strongest autoimmune/inflammatory risk
AA.detail: Homozygous A allele. Highest TNF-α gene transcription. Strongly elevated TNF-α levels. Associated with higher risk of rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel disease, septic shock, malaria severity, and response to anti-TNF biologics. Anti-TNF therapy (etanercept, adalimumab) is especially relevant for AA carriers with autoimmune disease.

description: TGF-β1 Leu10Pro codon 10 variant affecting TGF-β1 secretion and signalling in immune regulation and tumour suppression

CC.summary: Pro/Pro — higher TGF-β1 secretion
CC.detail: Homozygous Pro10 (T on some strand conventions). Higher TGF-β1 secretion efficiency. Stronger immunosuppressive signal. Some studies associate high TGF-β1 with increased cancer risk (poorer anti-tumour immunity) and fibrosis, while others note protection from autoimmune disease.

CT.detail: Heterozygous Leu10Pro. Intermediate TGF-β1 secretion. Balanced immune regulatory phenotype.

TT.summary: Leu/Leu — lower TGF-β1 secretion, altered immune balance
TT.detail: Homozygous Leu10. Lower TGF-β1 production. Reduced immunosuppressive signalling. Some studies associate lower TGF-β1 with increased autoimmune susceptibility (e.g. multiple sclerosis), while others suggest better anti-tumour immunity. Effects are context-dependent.

description: eNOS Glu298Asp variant (also known as NOS3 G894T) — affects endothelial nitric oxide synthase activity and vascular function

GG.summary: Normal eNOS activity — standard cardiovascular risk
GG.detail: Wild-type eNOS Glu298. Full endothelial nitric oxide production. Normal vascular tone, endothelial function, and standard cardiovascular risk from this locus.

GT.summary: Moderately reduced eNOS — slightly elevated CVD risk
GT.detail: Heterozygous Asp298 carrier. Slightly reduced NO production and eNOS activity. Modest elevation in hypertension and coronary artery disease risk. Standard lifestyle measures apply.

TT.summary: Reduced eNOS — higher hypertension and CVD risk
TT.detail: Homozygous Asp298. Substantially reduced eNOS activity and nitric oxide bioavailability. Associated with elevated hypertension risk, endothelial dysfunction, and increased risk of coronary artery disease and myocardial infarction. Exercise-induced NO production is especially beneficial for this genotype.

sources.name: PMID 9610379 — Hingorani et al. eNOS Glu298Asp

description: HLA-DQA1 variant tagging the DQ2.5 haplotype — the primary genetic prerequisite for celiac disease

CC.summary: Absent DQ2.5 haplotype — very low celiac risk
CC.detail: Does not carry the HLA-DQ2.5 haplotype (or low probability thereof). Extremely low genetic risk of celiac disease (~1-2% residual risk from DQ8 or other variants). Negative predictive value >99% for celiac disease.

CT.detail: Heterozygous T allele. Carries one copy of the DQ2.5-associated allele. Elevated celiac disease risk compared to population (~10-15% lifetime risk in susceptible populations, but most carriers never develop celiac). Gluten-related symptoms warrant serological testing.

TT.summary: DQ2.5 homozygote — highest genetic celiac risk
TT.detail: Homozygous T allele. DQ2.5/DQ2.5 genotype. Highest genetic risk — up to 30% lifetime celiac disease risk and higher risk of type 1 diabetes and other autoimmune disorders. Serological screening (tTG-IgA) recommended. Strict gluten avoidance upon diagnosis is the only effective treatment.

description: STAT4 intron 3 variant — one of the most replicated genetic risk loci shared across multiple autoimmune diseases

GG.detail: Wild-type STAT4. Standard Th1 immune signalling. No elevated risk of rheumatoid arthritis or SLE from this locus.

GT.detail: Heterozygous T carrier. Elevated STAT4 expression. ~1.5x increased risk of rheumatoid arthritis and ~1.6x for SLE. Also associated with type 1 diabetes, Sjögren's syndrome, and inflammatory bowel disease.

TT.summary: Highest STAT4-mediated autoimmune risk
TT.detail: Homozygous T allele. Strongest Th1 skewing via STAT4. ~2-3x elevated risk of rheumatoid arthritis and SLE. One of the most replicated cross-autoimmune risk loci — found in GWAS for RA, SLE, T1D, IBD, and other autoimmune diseases.

sources.name: PMID 17982569 — Remmers et al. STAT4 RA/SLE

description: SLCO1B1 intronic variant in strong linkage disequilibrium with rs4149056, independently associated with simvastatin-induced myopathy in the SEARCH trial

TT.summary: Normal statin transport — low myopathy risk
TT.detail: Wild-type. Normal hepatic uptake of statins via OATP1B1 transporter. Standard myopathy risk on statin therapy.

CT.summary: Reduced statin transport — moderate myopathy risk
CT.detail: Heterozygous carrier. Approximately 4-fold increased risk of simvastatin-induced myopathy. Consider lower statin dose or alternative statin with less OATP1B1 dependence (e.g. pravastatin, rosuvastatin).

CC.summary: Significantly impaired statin transport — high myopathy risk
CC.detail: Homozygous risk variant. Approximately 16-fold increased risk of simvastatin-induced myopathy. Strong recommendation to avoid high-dose simvastatin. Pharmacogenomic guidelines (CPIC) recommend alternative statins or reduced dosing.

sources.name: CPIC Guideline for Statins and SLCO1B1

description: ADH1B Arg48His variant causing rapid alcohol metabolism, strongly protective against alcohol dependence, common in East Asian populations (alcohol flush reaction)

CC.summary: Rapid alcohol metabolism — strong protection against alcoholism
CC.detail: Homozygous fast-metabolizer. Extremely rapid conversion of ethanol to acetaldehyde, causing facial flushing, nausea, and discomfort after alcohol consumption. Very strong protection against alcohol dependence (OR ~0.15). Most common in East Asian populations.

CT.summary: Moderately rapid alcohol metabolism — reduced alcoholism risk
CT.detail: Heterozygous carrier of the fast allele. Intermediate alcohol metabolism speed. Significant protection against alcohol dependence (OR ~0.4). May experience mild flushing with alcohol.

TT.summary: Standard alcohol metabolism
TT.detail: Wild-type (ancestral). Normal rate of ethanol to acetaldehyde conversion. No inherent protection from alcohol flush reaction. Standard risk for alcohol dependence from this variant.

description: ALDH2*2 (Glu504Lys) variant causing aldehyde dehydrogenase deficiency — the primary cause of Asian alcohol flush syndrome and elevated esophageal cancer risk when drinking

GG.summary: Normal aldehyde dehydrogenase — standard alcohol processing
GG.detail: Wild-type ALDH2. Normal clearance of acetaldehyde after alcohol consumption. No flush reaction from this variant. Standard esophageal cancer risk.

AG.summary: Reduced ALDH2 activity — alcohol flush, elevated cancer risk if drinking
AG.detail: Heterozygous ALDH2*2 carrier. Approximately 10-fold reduced enzyme activity. Accumulation of acetaldehyde causes facial flushing, tachycardia, and nausea after alcohol. 6–10x increased esophageal cancer risk if drinking regularly. Protective against alcoholism due to aversive reaction.

AA.summary: Near-absent ALDH2 activity — severe flush, high cancer risk if drinking
AA.detail: Homozygous ALDH2*2. Near-complete loss of aldehyde dehydrogenase activity. Severe adverse reaction to even small amounts of alcohol. Very high esophageal and head/neck cancer risk if alcohol is consumed. Strongly protective against alcohol dependence. Common in East Asian populations (~5–8%).

description: HERC2 regulatory variant upstream of OCA2 — the strongest single genetic determinant of blue vs. brown eye color in Europeans

AA.summary: Brown eyes most likely
AA.detail: Homozygous ancestral allele. High OCA2 expression leading to high melanin production in the iris. Brown eye color in virtually all cases. This is the predominant genotype in African, East Asian, and South Asian populations.

AG.summary: Variable eye color — likely brown or hazel/green
AG.detail: Heterozygous. Intermediate OCA2 expression. Eye color varies — most commonly brown or hazel, occasionally green. Blue eyes are uncommon with this genotype but not impossible depending on other pigmentation loci.

GG.detail: Homozygous derived allele. Reduced OCA2 transcription leading to decreased iris melanin. Blue eye color in ~72% of European GG carriers. Single most predictive SNP for blue eye color (explains ~75% of eye color variation in Europeans). Originated from a single common ancestor ~6,000–10,000 years ago.

description: Hepatic lipase (LIPC) promoter variant affecting HDL cholesterol levels — one of the most replicated HDL-associated loci in GWAS

CC.summary: Higher hepatic lipase activity — lower HDL cholesterol
CC.detail: Wild-type. Normal hepatic lipase expression and activity. Associated with lower HDL-C levels relative to T-allele carriers. Standard cardiovascular risk from this locus.

CT.summary: Intermediate hepatic lipase — moderately elevated HDL
CT.detail: Heterozygous carrier. Reduced hepatic lipase promoter activity leading to moderately higher HDL-C levels (~2–3 mg/dL increase). Modest cardiovascular benefit.

TT.summary: Reduced hepatic lipase — higher HDL cholesterol
TT.detail: Homozygous T allele. Significantly reduced hepatic lipase expression. Associated with higher HDL-C levels (~4–6 mg/dL increase). Mildly protective cardiovascular profile. One of the most robustly replicated HDL-raising variants across diverse populations.

description: MC1R R151C — one of the strongest red hair/fair skin variants, also associated with increased melanoma risk and altered pain sensitivity/anesthesia requirements

CC.summary: Normal MC1R function — standard pigmentation
CC.detail: Wild-type MC1R. Normal eumelanin production. Standard skin and hair pigmentation determined by other loci. Baseline melanoma risk from this variant.

CT.summary: Carrier — lighter skin, mildly increased melanoma risk
CT.detail: Heterozygous R151C carrier. Partial shift from eumelanin toward pheomelanin production. Tendency toward lighter skin, freckling, and sun sensitivity. Approximately 2-fold increased melanoma risk. May require ~20% more anesthesia.

TT.summary: Red hair likely — significantly increased melanoma risk
TT.detail: Homozygous R151C. Strongly reduced eumelanin production. Red hair and very fair skin phenotype highly likely (especially with other MC1R variants). Approximately 3–4 fold increased melanoma risk. Increased pain sensitivity and higher anesthetic requirements reported in multiple studies. UV protection strongly recommended.

description: NAT2 G590A (R197Q) — one of the most common slow acetylator alleles, affecting isoniazid metabolism, sulfonamide drug response, and aromatic amine carcinogen detoxification

GG.detail: Wild-type NAT2 at position 590. Normal N-acetyltransferase 2 activity from this site. Rapid acetylation of isoniazid and other aromatic amine substrates. Standard drug dosing appropriate. Note: full acetylator phenotype depends on combinations across multiple NAT2 SNPs.

AG.detail: Heterozygous carrier of the slow allele. Reduced NAT2 activity. Intermediate isoniazid metabolism — may benefit from dose adjustment during TB treatment. Slightly increased risk of drug-induced liver injury with standard isoniazid dosing.

AA.summary: Slow acetylator — higher drug toxicity risk
AA.detail: Homozygous slow allele. Significantly reduced NAT2 enzyme activity. Slow metabolism of isoniazid, hydralazine, procainamide, and sulfonamides. Higher risk of isoniazid-induced hepatotoxicity and peripheral neuropathy. CPIC guidelines recommend dose reduction. Also associated with increased bladder cancer risk from occupational aromatic amine exposure.

description: GSTP1 Ile105Val — common variant affecting glutathione S-transferase activity, platinum-based chemotherapy detoxification, and oxidative stress response

AA.summary: Normal GSTP1 enzyme activity
AA.detail: Wild-type Ile/Ile. Normal glutathione conjugation activity. Standard detoxification of platinum-based chemotherapy agents (cisplatin, carboplatin, oxaliplatin). Baseline response to oxidative stress.

AG.summary: Reduced GSTP1 activity — variable chemo response
AG.detail: Heterozygous Ile/Val. Moderately reduced GSTP1 catalytic activity. Some studies show improved response to platinum chemotherapy (slower drug clearance) but increased treatment-related toxicity. Variable findings across cancer types.

GG.summary: Low GSTP1 activity — altered chemo metabolism
GG.detail: Homozygous Val/Val. Substantially reduced glutathione S-transferase P1 activity. Decreased detoxification of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons and platinum drugs. Associated with improved platinum-chemotherapy efficacy in some cancers (lung, ovarian) but higher toxicity risk. Reduced capacity to neutralize oxidative stress.

sources.name: PMID 15956009 — GSTP1 and platinum chemo meta-analysis

description: IGF2BP2 intronic variant consistently associated with type 2 diabetes risk across multiple GWAS in diverse populations — affects insulin-like growth factor 2 mRNA-binding protein 2

GG.summary: Lower T2D risk from this locus
GG.detail: Wild-type. Normal IGF2BP2 expression and insulin-like growth factor signalling. Baseline type 2 diabetes risk from this variant.

GT.detail: Heterozygous carrier. Modest increase in type 2 diabetes risk (OR ~1.12–1.14 per allele). Effect is consistent across European, East Asian, and South Asian populations. Mechanism likely involves impaired pancreatic beta-cell function.

TT.summary: Moderately increased T2D risk
TT.detail: Homozygous risk allele. Approximately 1.25–1.30 fold increased risk of type 2 diabetes. One of the most robustly replicated T2D loci in GWAS. Lifestyle interventions (diet, exercise) can significantly attenuate genetic risk.

description: 9p21.3 locus variant near CDKN2B-AS1 (ANRIL) — one of the most significant and replicated coronary artery disease risk variants, independent of traditional lipid risk factors

AA.summary: Lower CAD risk from this locus
AA.detail: Homozygous protective allele. Lower coronary artery disease risk from the 9p21 locus. This effect is independent of cholesterol, blood pressure, and other traditional risk factors.

AG.detail: Heterozygous carrier. Moderately increased coronary artery disease risk (OR ~1.25). The 9p21 locus is one of the strongest and most replicated GWAS signals for CAD. Effect is mediated through vascular smooth muscle cell proliferation and inflammation, not lipid pathways.

GG.detail: Homozygous risk allele. Approximately 1.5–1.6 fold increased risk of coronary artery disease and myocardial infarction. Effect is consistent across ethnicities and independent of traditional cardiovascular risk factors. Cannot be mitigated by statins alone — lifestyle intervention (exercise, smoking cessation) is particularly important.

sources.name: PMID 17634449 — Wellcome Trust Case Control Consortium
PMID 17478679 — McPherson et al. 9p21 CAD locus

description: TCF7L2 intronic variant — second major TCF7L2 diabetes SNP, independently associated with impaired beta-cell function and type 2 diabetes risk across all studied populations

GG.summary: Lower T2D risk from this variant
GG.detail: Wild-type. Normal TCF7L2-mediated Wnt signalling in pancreatic beta cells. Baseline type 2 diabetes risk from this locus.

GT.detail: Heterozygous carrier. Approximately 1.35–1.45 fold increased type 2 diabetes risk. Impaired glucose-stimulated insulin secretion. TCF7L2 is the single strongest common genetic risk factor for T2D. Lifestyle modification significantly reduces penetrance.

TT.summary: Significantly increased T2D risk
TT.detail: Homozygous risk allele. Approximately 1.8–2.0 fold increased type 2 diabetes risk. Substantially impaired pancreatic beta-cell function. Despite being the strongest common T2D locus, risk is substantially modifiable through diet, exercise, and weight management.

description: OCA2 intronic variant — the second strongest eye color determinant after HERC2 rs12913832, contributing to blue/green/hazel eye color variation in Europeans

AA.summary: Reduced OCA2 expression — lighter eye color likely
AA.detail: Homozygous derived allele. Reduced OCA2 (P protein) expression in melanocytes. Contributes to lighter iris pigmentation (blue, green, or hazel). Effect is additive with HERC2 rs12913832 — individuals with derived alleles at both loci have the highest probability of blue eyes.

AG.summary: Intermediate OCA2 effect — variable eye color
AG.detail: Heterozygous. Intermediate OCA2 expression. Eye color depends heavily on HERC2 genotype and other pigmentation loci. Green, hazel, and light brown outcomes are common.

GG.summary: Full OCA2 expression — darker eye color likely
GG.detail: Wild-type. Full OCA2 expression supporting robust melanin synthesis in the iris. Brown eye color most likely, especially in combination with ancestral HERC2 genotype.

description: IL-10 promoter variant (-1082 A/G) affecting expression of the anti-inflammatory cytokine interleukin-10 — associated with autoimmune disease susceptibility and transplant outcomes

CC.summary: Higher IL-10 production — anti-inflammatory profile
CC.detail: Homozygous G allele (C on opposite strand). Higher constitutive and stimulated IL-10 production. Stronger anti-inflammatory response. Lower risk of certain autoimmune conditions (IBD, rheumatoid arthritis). May be associated with increased susceptibility to certain infections due to dampened immune activation.

CT.detail: Heterozygous. Intermediate IL-10 cytokine levels. Balanced pro/anti-inflammatory profile. Most common genotype in many populations.

TT.summary: Lower IL-10 production — pro-inflammatory profile
TT.detail: Homozygous A allele (T on opposite strand). Lower IL-10 production. Tendency toward stronger inflammatory responses. Increased risk of inflammatory bowel disease, rheumatoid arthritis, and graft rejection. May have more robust anti-infection immune responses.

description: ApoER2 (LRP8) R952Q variant associated with familial and early-onset myocardial infarction and platelet hyperreactivity

CC.summary: Standard ApoER2 function — baseline MI risk
CC.detail: Wild-type LRP8/ApoER2. Normal platelet activation and function. Baseline cardiovascular and thrombotic risk from this locus.

CT.detail: Heterozygous carrier of Q952 variant. Altered ApoER2 receptor function affecting platelet reactivity. Approximately 1.5-fold increased risk of myocardial infarction, especially premature MI (<45 years). Enhanced platelet aggregation response.

TT.summary: Higher MI and thrombosis risk
TT.detail: Homozygous Q952 variant. Significantly altered platelet signalling via ApoER2. Approximately 2-fold increased risk of early-onset myocardial infarction. Platelet hyperreactivity may contribute to arterial thrombosis. Antiplatelet therapy considerations are relevant.

description: 9p21 variant near CDKN2A/2B associated with type 2 diabetes risk through impaired pancreatic beta-cell proliferation — distinct from the nearby CAD-associated variants

CC.summary: Lower T2D risk from this locus
CC.detail: Homozygous protective allele. Normal CDKN2A/2B regulation of beta-cell cycle. Lower type 2 diabetes risk from this variant.

CT.detail: Heterozygous carrier. Modest increase in type 2 diabetes risk (OR ~1.15–1.20). Mechanism involves impaired pancreatic beta-cell regeneration via p15/p16 cell cycle inhibitor dysregulation. Effect is independent of the nearby CAD-associated 9p21 variants.

TT.summary: Moderately increased T2D risk
TT.detail: Homozygous risk allele. Approximately 1.35–1.40 fold increased type 2 diabetes risk. One of the first T2D GWAS loci replicated across European, East Asian, and African populations. Risk is modifiable through lifestyle interventions.

description: FOXO3 intronic variant — one of the most replicated human longevity-associated SNPs, linked to stress resistance, insulin/IGF-1 signalling, and cardiovascular health across multiple populations

GG.detail: Homozygous longevity allele. Associated with increased FOXO3 expression and enhanced cellular stress resistance, autophagy, and DNA repair. Significantly enriched in centenarians across Hawaiian, Japanese, German, Chinese, and Italian cohorts. Approximately 1.5–2.7 fold increased odds of living to 100. Improved insulin sensitivity and cardiovascular health markers.

GT.detail: Heterozygous carrier. Intermediate FOXO3 expression. Moderately enriched in long-lived individuals. Some benefits to stress resistance and metabolic health.

TT.summary: Common genotype — standard FOXO3 function
TT.detail: Wild-type (most common allele). Standard FOXO3 transcription factor activity and cellular stress response. No longevity association from this variant.

description: MTRR A66G (Ile22Met) — methionine synthase reductase variant affecting vitamin B12-dependent methionine regeneration and homocysteine metabolism, relevant to neural tube defect risk

AA.summary: Normal MTRR function
AA.detail: Wild-type Ile/Ile. Normal methionine synthase reductase activity. Efficient reactivation of vitamin B12-dependent methionine synthase. Standard homocysteine metabolism.

AG.detail: Heterozygous Ile/Met. Mildly reduced MTRR enzyme efficiency. Minimal effect on homocysteine levels in isolation. Risk increases when combined with other folate cycle variants (MTHFR C677T/A1298C) or low B12/folate status.

GG.summary: Reduced MTRR activity — elevated homocysteine risk
GG.detail: Homozygous Met/Met. Reduced methionine synthase reductase activity. Impaired reactivation of cobalamin-dependent methionine synthase. Can contribute to elevated homocysteine, especially with concurrent MTHFR variants or B12 deficiency. Associated with increased neural tube defect risk (OR ~1.5–2.0 in combination with low folate). Adequate B12 and folate intake is particularly important.

sources.name: PMID 15060097 — MTRR and neural tube defects

description: CFH Y402H — complement factor H variant; the T allele (His402) strongly increases risk for age-related macular degeneration (AMD) by impairing complement regulation in the eye

CC.summary: Lower AMD risk
CC.detail: Homozygous wild-type (Tyr/Tyr). Normal complement factor H function. Lowest risk for age-related macular degeneration. Complement regulation in the retina is intact.

CT.detail: Heterozygous (Tyr/His). Complement factor H activity partially reduced. AMD risk approximately 2–3× compared to CC. Regular ophthalmologic screening is recommended after age 50.

TT.summary: Substantially increased AMD risk
TT.detail: Homozygous risk allele (His/His). Severely impaired complement regulation in the retina. AMD risk approximately 5–7× compared to CC genotype. Among the strongest genetic risk factors for AMD. Smoking further amplifies risk. Routine screening and lifestyle measures (AREDS supplementation, no smoking) are strongly recommended.

sources.name: Klein et al. 2005 — CFH and AMD (Science)

description: ARMS2 A69S — age-related maculopathy susceptibility 2 variant; the T allele strongly increases risk for age-related macular degeneration and acts independently of CFH Y402H

GG.detail: Homozygous wild-type. Normal ARMS2 function. Lowest AMD risk attributable to this locus. ARMS2 protein localizes to mitochondria; wild-type may support retinal cell health.

GT.detail: Heterozygous carrier. AMD risk approximately 2.5–3× compared to GG. Combination with CFH rs1061170 TT substantially compounds risk. Regular retinal examinations recommended after age 50.

TT.summary: Substantially increased AMD risk
TT.detail: Homozygous risk allele. AMD risk approximately 8–10× compared to GG, one of the highest single-SNP ORs in complex disease genetics. When combined with CFH Y402H TT, lifetime AMD risk exceeds 50%. AREDS supplements, anti-VEGF awareness, and frequent monitoring are advised.

sources.name: Jakobsdottir et al. 2005 — ARMS2 and AMD

description: ABCG2 Q141K — urate transporter variant; the T allele reduces renal and gut uric acid secretion, raising serum urate and substantially increasing gout risk

GG.detail: Homozygous wild-type (Gln/Gln). Full ABCG2 urate transporter activity. Normal serum urate excretion. Lowest ABCG2-attributable gout risk.

GT.detail: Heterozygous (Gln/Lys). ~53% reduction in ABCG2 transport function. Serum urate mildly elevated. Gout risk approximately 1.7× compared to GG. Risk amplified by high-purine diet, alcohol, and diuretic use.

TT.summary: Substantially impaired urate excretion — high gout risk
TT.detail: Homozygous risk allele (Lys/Lys). ~97% loss of ABCG2 transport function. Markedly elevated serum urate. Gout risk approximately 3–4× compared to GG. Particularly prevalent in East Asian populations (T allele ~0.30). Dietary purine restriction, adequate hydration, and urate-lowering therapy may be indicated if symptomatic.

sources.name: Matsuo et al. 2009 — ABCG2 and gout (Science Translational Medicine)

description: SLC2A9 intronic variant — one of the strongest GWAS signals for serum uric acid levels; the T allele is associated with lower urate, acting as a protective allele against gout

CC.summary: Higher serum urate
CC.detail: Homozygous reference. Associated with relatively higher serum uric acid levels. SLC2A9 (GLUT9) transports urate in the kidney; the C allele is linked to less efficient urate reabsorption reduction, resulting in higher circulating urate.

CT.detail: Heterozygous. Serum urate intermediate between CC and TT. Modest protective effect against hyperuricemia and gout.

TT.summary: Lower serum urate — reduced gout risk
TT.detail: Homozygous T allele. Strongly associated with lower serum uric acid levels (effect ~0.2–0.3 mg/dL per allele in meta-analyses). Reduced risk of hyperuricemia and gout. Among the most replicated urate-lowering associations in GWAS.

sources.name: Köttgen et al. 2013 — Urate GWAS meta-analysis (Nature Genetics)

description: DPYD*2A (IVS14+1G>A) — splice-site variant abolishing dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase activity; the T allele causes severe 5-fluorouracil / capecitabine toxicity and is an actionable pharmacogenomic marker

CC.summary: Normal DPYD function — standard 5-FU dosing
CC.detail: Homozygous wild-type. Normal dihydropyrimidine dehydrogenase (DPD) enzyme activity. Standard fluoropyrimidine (5-FU, capecitabine) dosing is appropriate. No DPYD*2A–related toxicity concern.

CT.summary: Reduced DPYD activity — high toxicity risk
CT.detail: Heterozygous DPYD*2A carrier. DPD activity reduced by approximately 50%. Fluoropyrimidine dose reduction of 50% is recommended by CPIC and DPWG guidelines before initiating treatment. Severe or life-threatening toxicity (mucositis, myelosuppression, neurotoxicity) risk is substantially elevated at standard doses.

TT.summary: Absent DPYD activity — fluoropyrimidines contraindicated
TT.detail: Homozygous DPYD*2A. Complete absence of DPD enzyme activity. Fluoropyrimidine use is contraindicated or requires exceptional dose reduction with close monitoring. Extremely rare but uniformly associated with life-threatening toxicity at standard doses. Alternative chemotherapy regimens should be considered.

sources.name: CPIC Guideline for Fluoropyrimidines and DPYD
Deenen et al. 2016 — DPYD*2A prospective trial (JCO)

description: AGT M235T — angiotensinogen variant; the G allele (Thr235) is associated with higher circulating angiotensinogen levels and increased hypertension risk

AA.summary: Lower angiotensinogen — reduced hypertension risk
AA.detail: Homozygous wild-type (Met/Met). Lower plasma angiotensinogen levels. Lowest AGT-attributable hypertension risk. Normal renin-angiotensin-aldosterone system (RAAS) tone.

AG.detail: Heterozygous (Met/Thr). Mildly elevated angiotensinogen. Modestly increased hypertension risk. Most common genotype globally (G allele frequency ~0.54). RAAS activity slightly elevated.

GG.detail: Homozygous Thr235. Higher plasma angiotensinogen concentrations drive increased angiotensin II production. Associated with ~1.3–1.5× increased hypertension risk. Effect magnified with high dietary sodium intake and obesity. ACE inhibitor and ARB therapies target this pathway directly.

sources.name: Jeunemaitre et al. 1992 — AGT and hypertension (Cell)

description: CACNA1C intronic variant — L-type voltage-gated calcium channel gene; the A allele is one of the most replicated GWAS signals for bipolar disorder and schizophrenia risk

GG.detail: Homozygous wild-type. Lowest CACNA1C-attributable risk for bipolar disorder and schizophrenia. Normal calcium channel regulation in neuronal signaling.

AG.detail: Heterozygous. A allele associated with modest increase in bipolar disorder and schizophrenia risk (OR ~1.15 per allele in large GWAS meta-analyses). Altered CACNA1C expression may affect hippocampal and prefrontal calcium-mediated signaling. Risk is polygenic — this SNP contributes a small fraction of overall liability.

AA.summary: Moderately increased psychiatric risk
AA.detail: Homozygous risk allele. Additive effect approximately doubling the per-allele OR. Associated with higher CACNA1C expression and altered neuronal excitability. Notably rare in East Asian populations (~0.05) but common in European (~0.33) and African (~0.40) populations. Psychiatric risk is complex and multifactorial; this SNP alone does not determine outcome.

description: PCSK9 R46L — loss-of-function variant in proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9; the T allele reduces LDL-cholesterol by ~15% and substantially lowers coronary heart disease risk

GG.detail: Homozygous wild-type. Normal PCSK9 function — LDL receptors are degraded at a typical rate. Standard LDL-cholesterol levels. No PCSK9 R46L–related protection.

GT.summary: Reduced PCSK9 — lower LDL and CHD risk
GT.detail: Heterozygous R46L carrier. Partial loss of PCSK9 function. LDL-cholesterol reduced by approximately 15%. Coronary heart disease risk reduced by approximately 28% (Mendelian randomization data from large biobanks). Fewer LDL receptors are degraded, improving hepatic LDL clearance.

TT.summary: Substantially reduced PCSK9 — very low LDL
TT.detail: Homozygous R46L. Near-complete loss of PCSK9 function at this locus. LDL-cholesterol substantially reduced. This genotype is extremely rare (allele frequency ~0.013 globally, ~0.017 in Europeans). Individuals with naturally very low LDL due to PCSK9 loss-of-function variants have provided the biological rationale for the PCSK9 inhibitor drug class (alirocumab, evolocumab).

sources.name: Cohen et al. 2006 — PCSK9 and CHD (NEJM)

description: CETP TaqIB (rs708272) — cholesteryl ester transfer protein intronic variant; the A allele (B2) is associated with higher HDL-cholesterol due to reduced CETP activity

GG.detail: Homozygous B1/B1. Normal CETP activity. Average HDL-cholesterol levels. CETP transfers cholesterol esters from HDL to LDL and VLDL; higher CETP activity lowers HDL.

AG.summary: Mildly reduced CETP — slightly elevated HDL
AG.detail: Heterozygous B1/B2. Moderately reduced CETP activity. HDL-cholesterol slightly elevated compared to GG. Modest cardiovascular benefit observed in epidemiological studies.

AA.summary: Low CETP activity — elevated HDL
AA.detail: Homozygous B2/B2. Substantially reduced CETP activity. HDL-cholesterol significantly elevated (by ~5–10% on average). Associated with reduced risk of coronary artery disease in observational studies. Note: pharmacological CETP inhibition trials have had mixed cardiovascular outcomes, suggesting the SNP effect reflects a different mechanism or threshold.

sources.name: Boekholdt et al. 2005 — CETP TaqIB meta-analysis (JAMA)

description: HCP5 rs2395029 — proxy marker for HLA-B*57:01; the G allele predicts abacavir hypersensitivity syndrome (AHS), a potentially life-threatening immune reaction to this HIV antiretroviral

TT.summary: HLA-B*57:01 unlikely — abacavir can be used
TT.detail: Homozygous wild-type. Very low probability of carrying HLA-B*57:01. Abacavir hypersensitivity syndrome (AHS) is unlikely. Standard HIV treatment protocols apply, though confirmatory HLA-B*57:01 genotyping remains the clinical gold standard before prescribing abacavir.

GT.detail: Heterozygous. Strong proxy for HLA-B*57:01 heterozygosity. Clinically significant risk of abacavir hypersensitivity syndrome. AHS occurs in ~5–8% of abacavir-treated patients carrying HLA-B*57:01 and can be life-threatening (fever, rash, multi-organ involvement). Confirmatory HLA-B*57:01 testing and avoidance of abacavir is recommended.

GG.detail: Homozygous G allele. Very high probability of HLA-B*57:01 homozygosity. Abacavir is contraindicated. AHS risk is near-certain at standard doses. Alternative antiretroviral regimens should be prescribed. This genotype is extremely rare in the general population.

sources.name: CPIC Guideline for Abacavir and HLA-B
Martin et al. 2004 — HCP5 and HLA-B*57:01 (JID)

description: CLOCK 3111T/C (rs1801260) — 3'UTR variant in the core circadian clock gene; the G allele (C strand) is associated with delayed sleep phase, eveningness, and altered circadian rhythm

AA.summary: Morningness tendency — earlier sleep timing
AA.detail: Homozygous T allele (on sense strand). Associated with morning chronotype (earlier sleep and wake times). Normal circadian period length. Lower risk of delayed sleep phase disorder.

AG.detail: Heterozygous. Intermediate chronotype between morning and evening preference. Mild circadian variation. Some studies associate the G allele with slightly later preferred sleep timing.

GG.detail: Homozygous G allele (C on sense strand). Associated with evening chronotype, preference for later sleep and wake times, and longer sleep onset latency. Associated with delayed sleep phase disorder and insomnia in multiple studies. Social jetlag (misalignment between internal and societal clocks) may be more pronounced. Light therapy and sleep hygiene are particularly beneficial.

sources.name: Katzenberg et al. 1998 — CLOCK and diurnal preference (Sleep)

description: UGT1A1 upstream variant (proxy for TA-repeat promoter) — associated with reduced UGT1A1 expression; the T allele (minor) marks reduced bilirubin glucuronidation causing Gilbert syndrome and elevated irinotecan toxicity risk

CC.summary: Normal UGT1A1 activity
CC.detail: Homozygous wild-type. Normal UGT1A1 glucuronosyltransferase activity. Normal bilirubin conjugation and clearance. Standard irinotecan metabolism — SN-38 (active metabolite) is efficiently glucuronidated. No Gilbert syndrome.

CT.summary: Mildly reduced UGT1A1 — possible mild hyperbilirubinemia
CT.detail: Heterozygous. Moderately reduced UGT1A1 promoter activity. Mild unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia possible, especially during fasting or illness. Irinotecan dose adjustment may be warranted at high doses — elevated SN-38 exposure risk.

TT.summary: Reduced UGT1A1 — Gilbert syndrome and irinotecan toxicity risk
TT.detail: Homozygous T allele. Substantially reduced UGT1A1 expression. Associated with Gilbert syndrome (benign intermittent jaundice, especially with fasting, illness, or exercise). Elevated unconjugated bilirubin. Significantly increased irinotecan toxicity risk (severe neutropenia, diarrhea) due to impaired SN-38 glucuronidation. CPIC and FDA label recommend dose reduction or alternative chemotherapy. Gilbert syndrome itself is benign but the pharmacogenomic implications are clinically important.

sources.name: CPIC Guideline for Irinotecan and UGT1A1

description: SOD2 Val16Ala (rs4880) — manganese superoxide dismutase mitochondrial targeting signal variant; the G allele (Ala) allows more efficient mitochondrial import of MnSOD, altering reactive oxygen species metabolism

AA.summary: Less efficient MnSOD — higher oxidative stress
AA.detail: Homozygous Val/Val. MnSOD protein has a less efficient mitochondrial targeting sequence. Reduced mitochondrial antioxidant capacity. Associated with higher levels of oxidative DNA damage. Some studies link to increased breast, prostate, and lung cancer risk when combined with low antioxidant intake.

AG.detail: Heterozygous Val/Ala. Intermediate mitochondrial MnSOD import efficiency. Moderate oxidative stress buffering capacity. Most common genotype (G allele frequency ~0.47 globally).

GG.detail: Homozygous Ala/Ala. Highly efficient mitochondrial targeting of MnSOD. Enhanced superoxide dismutation in mitochondria. Better protection against oxidative stress. However, very high MnSOD activity may paradoxically increase hydrogen peroxide levels, requiring adequate catalase and glutathione peroxidase activity for full benefit.

sources.name: Sutton et al. 2006 — SOD2 Val16Ala and cancer risk meta-analysis

description: GC Asp432Glu (rs7041) — vitamin D binding protein (DBP) variant that defines Gc1s/Gc2 isoforms; alters vitamin D transport and bioavailability, one of the strongest GWAS signals for circulating 25(OH)D levels

AA.summary: Gc1f/Gc1f — higher DBP affinity for vitamin D
AA.detail: Homozygous reference (Asp/Asp). Higher affinity vitamin D binding protein. Total 25(OH)D levels tend to be higher but bioavailable (free) vitamin D may be comparable. This isoform is most common in African-descent populations.

AC.detail: Heterozygous (Asp/Glu). Intermediate vitamin D binding protein affinity. Circulating 25(OH)D levels intermediate between AA and CC genotypes. Most common genotype globally.

CC.summary: Gc2/Gc2 — lower DBP affinity, lower total 25(OH)D
CC.detail: Homozygous Glu/Glu (Gc2 isoform). Lower affinity for 25(OH)D and 1,25(OH)₂D. Associated with lower total circulating 25(OH)D levels (~2–4 nmol/L per allele in GWAS). However, bioavailable vitamin D may be adequate despite lower total levels. Vitamin D supplementation may appear less effective when monitoring total 25(OH)D. This isoform is more common in European populations.

sources.name: Ahn et al. 2010 — GC polymorphisms and vitamin D (Lancet)

description: NQO1 P187S (rs1800566) — NAD(P)H quinone dehydrogenase 1 variant; the A allele (Ser187) destabilizes the enzyme, abolishing NQO1 detoxification activity and increasing susceptibility to benzene toxicity and certain cancers

GG.detail: Homozygous wild-type (Pro/Pro). Full NQO1 enzyme activity. Efficient two-electron reduction of quinones, preventing redox cycling and oxidative damage. Normal benzene detoxification. Protective against quinone-mediated genotoxicity.

AG.detail: Heterozygous (Pro/Ser). NQO1 enzyme activity reduced to approximately 2–4× lower than wild-type. Partially impaired quinone detoxification. Modestly increased susceptibility to benzene hematotoxicity. Intermediate cancer risk for NQO1-substrate-related exposures.

AA.summary: Absent NQO1 activity — impaired detoxification
AA.detail: Homozygous Ser/Ser. NQO1 protein is rapidly degraded via the ubiquitin-proteasome pathway. Complete loss of NQO1 enzyme activity. Significantly increased risk of benzene-induced hematotoxicity and therapy-related acute myeloid leukemia. Associated with increased risk of bladder, lung, and colorectal cancers in some populations. The A allele is particularly common in East Asian populations (~0.45). Relevant for occupational benzene exposure risk assessment.

sources.name: Luo et al. 2014 — NQO1 P187S and cancer risk meta-analysis

description: IL1B +3954C>T (rs1143634) — interleukin-1 beta exonic variant; the A allele is associated with higher IL-1β secretion and enhanced pro-inflammatory response

GG.detail: Homozygous wild-type. Standard interleukin-1 beta production in response to inflammatory stimuli. Normal innate immune signaling. Lower baseline inflammatory tone attributable to this locus.

AG.detail: Heterozygous. Approximately 2× higher IL-1β production compared to GG in monocyte stimulation assays. Mildly elevated systemic inflammation. Associated with modestly increased risk of periodontitis, gastric cancer, and coronary artery disease in some populations.

AA.summary: Elevated IL-1β — enhanced inflammatory response
AA.detail: Homozygous risk allele. Approximately 4× higher IL-1β production compared to GG. Enhanced pro-inflammatory response to pathogens but also to sterile inflammatory triggers. Associated with increased risk of periodontitis, gastric cancer (in H. pylori carriers), and inflammatory bowel disease. Anti-inflammatory dietary patterns and IL-1 pathway–targeted therapeutics (anakinra, canakinumab) are relevant.

sources.name: Pociot et al. 1992 — IL1B TaqI RFLP and IL-1β production

description: DRD2 C957T (rs6277) — synonymous variant in dopamine receptor D2 that alters mRNA stability; the A allele (T on coding strand) reduces D2 receptor density in the striatum, affecting reward processing and executive function

GG.summary: Higher D2 receptor density — stable mRNA
GG.detail: Homozygous C957 (coding strand C/C). DRD2 mRNA is more stable, leading to higher dopamine D2 receptor density in the striatum. Associated with better working memory performance under low-stress conditions. May be protective against substance use disorders.

AG.detail: Heterozygous (C/T on coding strand). Intermediate DRD2 mRNA stability and receptor density. Balanced dopamine signaling. Most common genotype in most populations.

AA.summary: Lower D2 receptor density — altered reward processing
AA.detail: Homozygous T957 (coding strand T/T). Reduced DRD2 mRNA stability leading to lower D2 receptor density. Associated with blunted reward sensitivity and potentially greater vulnerability to addictive behaviors. Some studies report increased risk of alcohol dependence and compulsive gambling. May show altered response to antipsychotic medications. Cognitive effects are context-dependent — may perform better under stress but worse under baseline conditions.

sources.name: Duan et al. 2003 — DRD2 C957T and mRNA stability

description: PTPN22 R620W (rs2476601) — protein tyrosine phosphatase non-receptor type 22 gain-of-function variant; the A allele disrupts T-cell receptor signaling calibration and is one of the strongest non-HLA genetic risk factors for multiple autoimmune diseases
